It also shows that ribosomal RNA sequences are conserved across these groups. | Codon | Amino Acid | Human | Yeast | *E. coli* | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | UUU | Phenylalanine | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | rRNA gene

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A1.2.10 Conservation of the genetic code as evidence of universal common ancestry
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question uses conservation of codon meaning (UUU → phenylalanine) across humans, yeast and E. coli to infer universal common ancestry via the conserved genetic code.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A4.1 Evolution and speciation > A4.1.2 Evidence for evolution from base sequences in DNA or RNA and amino acid sequences
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A4.1.2 Evidence for evolution from base sequences in DNA or RNA and amino acid sequences
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It also cites conserved rRNA gene regions across diverse taxa as molecular sequence evidence supporting evolution/common descent.

options.content: Independent evolution of the same genetic code in different lineages
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The near-universality of the genetic code is better explained by inheritance from a common ancestor than by multiple independent origins.

options.content: A single ancestral organism from which all life evolved
options.markscheme: Correct. Shared codons and conserved rRNA sequences across diverse organisms support common ancestry.

options.content: Horizontal gene transfer as the main reason all organisms share these sequences
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Horizontal gene transfer occurs, but it does not best explain the widespread conservation of core features such as rRNA across all domains.

options.content: Recent divergence of humans and bacteria from a common ancestor
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Humans and bacteria share a common ancestor, but their divergence is ancient, not recent.

The broth remained clear in both sealed and unsealed flasks as long as it was kept at a constant temperature.

questions.specification: In Louis Pasteur’s experiments, what was the primary purpose of using a flask with a swan-neck shape?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.1 Origins of cells (HL) > A2.1.3 Challenge of explaining the spontaneous origin of cells
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.3 Challenge of explaining the spontaneous origin of cells
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Louis Pasteur's swan-neck flask experiment is the classic historical evidence used to disprove the theory of spontaneous generation. This is a foundational concept in the 'Origins of Cells' topic, specifically addressing the challenge of how cells first appeared by demonstrating that life does not spontaneously arise from non-living matter under current environmental conditions.

options.content: To prevent the 'vital force' in the atmosphere from interacting with the broth.
To allow air to circulate while trapping microorganisms in the curve of the neck.
To increase the boiling point of the broth to ensure complete sterilization.
To provide a large surface area for the spontaneous assembly of organic molecules.

questions.specification: ![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d8121ec5-f489-414d-a895-de510e0e633b)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.1 Origins of cells (HL) > A2.1.1 Conditions on early Earth and the pre-biotic formation of carbon compounds (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.1 Conditions on early Earth and the pre-biotic formation of carbon compounds (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks how hydrothermal vents could provide conditions for prebiotic synthesis of organic compounds on early Earth, which directly matches early-Earth conditions and pre-biotic formation of carbon compounds.

parts.content: With reference to the process of prebiotic synthesis shown in the diagram, explain how hydrothermal vents could have provided the necessary conditions for the synthesis of organic compounds.
parts.markscheme:
- Hydrothermal vents provide high temperatures which provide activation energy for chemical reactions. <marks>1</marks>
- Hydrothermal vents release chemical compounds/elements such as hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$), methane ($\text{CH}_4$), and ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$). <marks>1</marks>
- These compounds could have reacted to form simple organic compounds such as amino acids or simple sugars. <marks>1</marks>
- Mineral surfaces in hydrothermal vents could have acted as catalysts to increase the rate of organic synthesis reactions. <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Map the heat source
steps.content: Looking at the diagram, we see superheated water at $350^{\circ}\text{C}$ emerging from the vent. In the context of prebiotic synthesis, this intense thermal energy is crucial because it provides the **activation energy** required for chemical reactions to occur between inorganic molecules. Without this heat, the molecules in the cold $2^{\circ}\text{C}$ surrounding seawater would not have enough energy to react and form new bonds.

highlights.text: high temperatures which provide activation energy

steps.title: Identify the raw materials
steps.content: The diagram labels several gases being released: hydrogen ($H_2$), methane ($CH_4$), and ammonia ($NH_3$). These represent the essential chemical building blocks (carbon, nitrogen, and hydrogen) needed for life. These reduced gases create a local environment where the synthesis of carbon compounds is thermodynamically favorable, mimicking the conditions hypothesized in the Miller-Urey experiment but in a deep-sea setting.

steps.title: Locate the catalytic surfaces
steps.content: The physical structure of the "chimney" is composed of various minerals. These **mineral surfaces** are not just containers; they act as biological catalysts. By providing a solid surface, they can concentrate the dissolved gases and ions, bringing them close together and orienting them in ways that increase the rate of reaction, much like enzymes do in modern cells.

highlights.text: Mineral surfaces in hydrothermal vents could have acted as catalysts

steps.title: Synthesize simple organic compounds
steps.content: When we map these conditions together—the energy from the heat, the raw material from the gases, and the efficiency from the mineral catalysts—the result is the synthesis of **simple organic compounds**. As shown at the top of the diagram, these inorganic components react to form molecules like amino acids or simple sugars, which are the foundational "building blocks" of life. This process is covered in **Topic A2.1: Origins of Cells**.

highlights.text: reacted to form simple organic compounds such as amino acids or simple sugars

steps.title: Introduction to Prebiotic Synthesis
steps.content:
To understand how life could begin, we look at **prebiotic synthesis**—the process of making organic molecules from non-living matter. This topic is covered in **Topic A2.1: Origins of cells (HL)**. Using the **Chemical Requirements Framework**, we can break down the conditions at hydrothermal vents into three essential components: 
1. **Inorganic precursors** (the building blocks)
2. **Activation energy** (the spark)
3. **Catalysts** (the helpers)

steps.title: Supply of Inorganic Precursors
steps.content:
The first requirement for any chemical reaction is the reactants. Hydrothermal vents act as a plumbing system for the early Earth, spewing out various inorganic gases and elements into the surrounding water. 

These vents release a rich supply of "building blocks" such as:
*   Hydrogen ($$H_2$$)
*   Methane ($$CH_4$$)
*   Ammonia ($$NH_3$$)

These simple molecules contain the carbon, nitrogen, and hydrogen atoms necessary to build more complex organic structures.

steps.title: Providing Activation Energy
steps.content:
Chemical reactions don't just happen spontaneously; they need a push to overcome the energy barrier known as **activation energy**. 

Hydrothermal vents provide intense heat from the Earth's interior. This thermal energy increases the kinetic energy of the molecules, allowing them to collide with enough force to break old bonds and form new ones. This high-temperature environment is a perfect "engine" for driving these otherwise difficult reactions.

steps.title: Mineral Surfaces as Catalysts
steps.content:
Even with heat, some reactions are too slow. This is where **catalysts** come in. The structure of a hydrothermal vent is made of various minerals and porous rocks. 

These mineral surfaces can act as natural catalysts. They provide a physical location where precursor molecules can cling to and concentrate, effectively lowering the activation energy required for the reaction and significantly increasing the rate of organic synthesis.

steps.title: Formation of Organic Compounds
steps.content:
When you combine these three factors—the **precursors** ($$H_2, CH_4, NH_3$$), the **activation energy** (heat), and the **catalysts** (mineral surfaces)—complex chemistry occurs. 

The result is the synthesis of simple organic compounds, such as **amino acids** (the building blocks of proteins) or **simple sugars**. This matches the process shown in your diagram, where inorganic gases are transformed into the organic molecules necessary for the first cells.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.9 Evidence for the evolution of the last universal common ancestor

parts.content: Outline what fossil evidence reveals about early life forms.
parts.markscheme:
- Fossil evidence includes stromatolites (layered microbial mats) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stromatolites provide evidence of photosynthetic prokaryotes/microbial communities in early Earth environments <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These fossils date back to approximately 3.5 billion years ago <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fossils indicate early life was primarily simple, prokaryotic and aquatic <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how molecular data can be used to infer evolutionary relationships among early life forms.
parts.markscheme:
- Analysis of ribosomal RNA sequences shows common ancestry among all living organisms <marks>1 mark</marks>
- rRNA genes are highly conserved, allowing comparisons across distantly related taxa <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Genetic sequence comparisons between modern organisms allow reconstruction of evolutionary relationships (phylogenies) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Molecular clocks using mutation rates can estimate divergence times for early lineages <marks>1 mark</marks>
- These molecular analyses can be used to trace lineages back to early prokaryotes <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how fossil evidence and molecular data together provide insight into the evolution of early life forms.
parts.markscheme:
- Fossils such as stromatolites provide direct physical evidence for early microbial life in ancient environments <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The age of these fossils (about 3.5 billion years) indicates life evolved relatively soon after Earth became habitable <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fossil morphology and distribution suggest early life was predominantly prokaryotic and formed communities/mats <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ribosomal RNA sequence comparisons show that all organisms share homologous genes, indicating common ancestry <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This molecular evidence supports the theory of universal common descent <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Phylogenetic analyses based on DNA/RNA/protein sequences reconstruct branching relationships among major lineages (e.g. bacteria and archaea) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Combining molecular phylogenies with the fossil timeline supports evolution from simple prokaryotic cells followed by diversification into the variety of life forms seen today <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for other relevant points about fossil or molecular evidence that provide insight into early life evolution</note>

parts.content: Outline how the formation of a cell membrane would have been a critical step in the evolution of living cells.
parts.markscheme:
- Formation of a distinct internal environment / separation from external environment. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allowed for the establishment of different chemical conditions inside the cell compared to outside. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enabled the concentration of specific molecules / reactants necessary for biochemical reactions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provided a protective barrier against harmful external substances / conditions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shielded delicate internal components from the harsh external environment. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Facilitated the regulation / selective passage of substances into and out of the cell. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allowed for the development and compartmentalization of metabolic pathways. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enabled the generation and storage of energy (e.g., proton gradients). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Essential for maintaining cellular homeostasis / stable internal conditions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for other relevant points related to membrane importance in cell evolution</note>

questions.specification:
The figure shows a schematic diagram of a possible mechanism by which organic biomorphs (primitive pre-cellular structures) may have formed in a Miller-Urey-type simulation. The Miller-Urey experiment recreated early Earth conditions, including an alkaline soup rich in gases like $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{NH}_3$, and $\text{N}_2$, exposed to electrical sparks mimicking lightning. This setup produced organic molecules like $\text{HCN}$ (hydrogen cyanide), which accumulated over time and self-organized into spherical, caterpillar-like, and polyp-like protocells through polymerization processes.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/tVeTj2J-AMT_rCQIy2Nrr.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.1 Origins of cells (HL) > A2.1.1 Conditions on early Earth and the pre-biotic formation of carbon compounds (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.1 Conditions on early Earth and the pre-biotic formation of carbon compounds (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (b) focus on early Earth atmospheric conditions and pre-biotic synthesis of carbon compounds as modeled by Miller–Urey, matching this HL origin-of-life condition topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.1 Origins of cells (HL) > A2.1.4 Evidence for the origin of carbon compounds
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.4 Evidence for the origin of carbon compounds
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) explicitly asks to evaluate the Miller–Urey experiment as evidence for the origin of carbon compounds, directly aligning with this evidence-focused leaf.

parts.content: State two conditions shown in the figure that made abiotic synthesis of organic (carbon) compounds possible in the Miller-Urey experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- reducing/organic-poor atmosphere containing gases such as $\text{CH}_4$, $\text{NH}_3$, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$ and water vapour $\text{H}_2\text{O(v)}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- electrical energy input from sparks/lightning ($\Delta E$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Any two conditions shown in the figure.</note>

parts.content: Based on the figure, describe the role of hydrogen cyanide (HCN) in forming early prebiotic structures.
parts.markscheme:
- $\text{HCN}$ produced/accumulates in the alkaline soup/at the gas–liquid interface <marks>1 mark</marks>
- polymerizes/condenses to form a surface organic film (SOF / poly-HCN) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- SOF can encapsulate/coat bubbles or droplets, providing a boundary that allows formation of protocell-like structures <marks>1 mark</marks>
- continued accumulation/polymerization drives morphogenesis into different shapes over time <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 2 marks.</note>

parts.content: Compare biomorph morphologies A, B and C shown in the figure; give one distinguishing feature for each.
parts.markscheme:
- A: single spherical, bubble-like protocell (one rounded vesicle) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- B: chain/series of connected spherical units (“caterpillar-like” serial bubbles) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- C: elongated, polyp-like structure with a narrow neck/stalk and enlarged tip (overall teardrop/column shape) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A/B/C may be distinguished by number of connected compartments (A one, B multiple, C one elongated) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A/B/C may be distinguished by degree of elongation (A low, B low per unit but multiple, C high) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark per morphology; maximum 3 marks.</note>

parts.content: Discuss the Miller-Urey experiment as evidence for the abiotic synthesis of organic (carbon) compounds; include one strength and one limitation.
parts.markscheme:
- Strength: showed that organic molecules can be produced abiotically from simple gases/energy input under simulated early-Earth conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Strength: supports the plausibility of chemical evolution providing building blocks for life (e.g., amino acids/other organics) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitation: the gas mixture/conditions used may not accurately represent the full complexity or actual composition of early Earth’s atmosphere/oceans <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitation: producing simple organics does not demonstrate the formation of living cells or self-replicating systems <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 2 marks: one strength and one limitation.</note>

questions.specification:
In Louis Pasteur’s swan-neck flask experiment, nutrient broth was boiled to sterilise it. Air could enter the flask, but dust and microorganisms were trapped in the curved neck.

What was the purpose of the swan-necked flask design?

options.content: To prevent microbes in the air from reaching the broth while still allowing air to circulate
To prevent a ‘vital force’ in the air from interacting with the nutrient medium
To create an anaerobic environment that would inhibit the growth of aerobic bacteria
To ensure steam from boiling would condense and seal the flask completely

questions.specification: Which experiment provided evidence for the abiotic synthesis of organic molecules?

questions.specification:
The diagram shows a symbiotic relationship between two cells. What advantage of this symbiosis led to the development of complex eukaryotic cells?
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/08508c9a-9fac-4153-a022-c5e1dbd339fc)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.2 Cell structure > A2.2.12  Origin of eukaryotic cells by endosymbiosis (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.2.12  Origin of eukaryotic cells by endosymbiosis (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question describes a symbiotic relationship between two cells leading to the development of complex eukaryotic cells, which directly tests the endosymbiotic theory for the origin of eukaryotes (e.g., mitochondria/chloroplasts arising via metabolic cooperation).

options.markscheme: Endosymbiosis primarily provided an energetic/metabolic advantage; gene transfer can occur later but is not the key immediate advantage of the symbiosis.
The host and endosymbiont benefited from complementary metabolism (e.g., increased ATP production/efficient energy yield), supporting greater cellular complexity.
Membrane-bound organelles are a result of eukaryotic evolution; they are not a feature/advantage of the initial symbiotic relationship.
Increased ATP production is a specific outcome of metabolic cooperation rather than a distinct feature separate from it in this context.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.1 Origins of cells (HL) > A2.1.3 Challenge of explaining the spontaneous origin of cells
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.3 Challenge of explaining the spontaneous origin of cells
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Pasteur’s swan-neck flask experiment is central evidence refuting spontaneous generation, linking to the broader challenge of explaining the spontaneous origin of cells.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.2 Cell structure > A2.2.1 Cells as the basic structural unit of all living organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.2.1 Cells as the basic structural unit of all living organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks how the experiment influenced understanding of cell theory, especially the idea that cells (life) arise from pre-existing cells rather than spontaneously.

parts.content: Outline Pasteur’s swan-neck flask experiment and state the conclusion about spontaneous generation.
parts.markscheme:
• Used swan-neck flasks containing sterilized nutrient broth 1 mark
  • Curved neck allowed air in but trapped dust particles/microorganisms so broth remained sterile 1 mark
  • Conclusion: microorganisms did not arise by spontaneous generation; they came from pre-existing microorganisms (e.g. in dust/air) 1 mark
Award marks for correct experimental setup/controls and an explicit conclusion rejecting spontaneous generation.

parts.content: Explain how Pasteur’s results support the cell theory principle that cells arise from pre-existing cells.
parts.markscheme:
• No growth occurred unless microorganisms could enter the broth (e.g. after breaking/tilting the flask), showing growth requires introduction of existing cells/spores 1 mark
  • Therefore new cells observed in the broth must have come from division/growth of pre-existing cells, not from non-living matter 1 mark
Award marks for linking the experimental outcome to the statement “all cells come from pre-existing cells”.

questions.specification:
An experiment was conducted using dialysis tubing to model osmosis. Two bags, $M$ and $N$, were filled with different concentrations of sucrose solution and placed into separate beakers containing pure water.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-biology-new-b937cd96-9539-499d-a274-21f9907b8907-1771815806714/f16a08cc-9c49-4576-9756-b472fe5cc503.jpeg)

The following graph shows the change in mass of the bags recorded over a period of 60 minutes.

![graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-biology-new-b937cd96-9539-499d-a274-21f9907b8907-1771815806714/e61087dd-496d-4b16-96f4-3d8e29897941.jpeg)

Which of the following factors explain why the results for $M$ and $N$ differ?

I. Bag $M$ had a steeper initial concentration gradient than Bag $N$

II. Sucrose molecules diffused out of the bags into the beakers over time

III. The rate of osmosis decreases as the system approaches equilibrium

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.3 Water potential > D2.3.3 Water movement by osmosis into or out of cells
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.3.3 Water movement by osmosis into or out of cells
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Dialysis tubing experiment measuring mass change of sucrose solutions in pure water is a direct model of osmosis into/out of a compartment, i.e., water movement across a partially permeable membrane.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B2.1 Membranes and membrane transport > B2.1.5 Movement of water molecules across membranes by osmosis and the role of aquaporins
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B2.1.5 Movement of water molecules across membranes by osmosis and the role of aquaporins
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Explaining differing rates over time using initial concentration gradients and approach to equilibrium relates to mechanisms/rate of osmosis across membranes (water transport).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.3 Water potential > D2.3.2 Water movement from less concentrated to more concentrated solutions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.3.2 Water movement from less concentrated to more concentrated solutions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Statement I refers to differences in initial concentration gradient between solutions (less concentrated to more concentrated), driving different osmosis rates.

questions.specification:
The table below compares several structural and functional features of mitochondria with those of typical prokaryotic cells.

| Feature | Mitochondria | Prokaryotic cell |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| DNA structure | Circular | Circular |
| Ribosome size | 70S | 70S |
| Method of division | Binary fission | Binary fission |
| Location of ATP synthase | Inner membrane | Plasma membrane |

Which biological theory provides the most plausible explanation for the similarities shown in the table?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.2 Cell structure > A2.2.12  Origin of eukaryotic cells by endosymbiosis (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.2.12  Origin of eukaryotic cells by endosymbiosis (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which theory explains mitochondria sharing prokaryotic features (circular DNA, 70S ribosomes, binary fission, membrane ATP synthase), which is classic evidence for endosymbiosis and the origin of eukaryotic cells by endosymbiosis.

questions.specification:
The origin of the first cells from non-living matter (abiogenesis) is proposed to have occurred in several stages. Which row in the table correctly matches a specific stage of this process with the relevant experimental evidence or scientific observation?

| | Stage of Abiogenesis | Evidence / Observation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Synthesis of simple organic monomers | Production of amino acids in a simulated reducing atmosphere |
| **B** | Assembly of polymers from subunits | Discovery of 70S ribosomes and circular DNA in mitochondria |
| **C** | Development of inheritance mechanisms | Pasteur’s experiments using sterilized swan-neck flasks |
| **D** | Packaging of molecules into membranes | The universal nature of the genetic code across all domains of life |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.1 Origins of cells (HL) > A2.1.4 Evidence for the origin of carbon compounds
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.4 Evidence for the origin of carbon compounds
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Row A refers to experimental production of amino acids in a simulated early Earth reducing atmosphere (Miller–Urey-type evidence), which is evidence for pre-biotic synthesis of carbon compounds.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.1 Origins of cells (HL) > A2.1.3 Challenge of explaining the spontaneous origin of cells
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.3 Challenge of explaining the spontaneous origin of cells
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is framed around stages of abiogenesis/origin of first cells and matching stages to evidence, which broadly targets understanding of proposed steps in spontaneous origin of cells.

options.markscheme: The Miller-Urey experiment demonstrated that simple organic molecules, such as amino acids, could be synthesized from inorganic precursors in a reducing atmosphere.
The presence of 70S ribosomes in mitochondria is evidence for the endosymbiotic theory, which explains the origin of eukaryotic cells, not the initial assembly of polymers during abiogenesis.
Pasteur’s experiments provided evidence for biogenesis (cells arising from pre-existing cells) and disproved spontaneous generation; they do not explain how the first inheritance mechanisms evolved.

The universal nature of the genetic code is evidence that all extant life shares a common ancestor, but it does not provide evidence for the physical packaging of molecules into membranes (protocells).

questions.specification:
The following table and diagram compare the genetic and protein-synthesis machinery of three different cellular components.

| Feature | Eukaryotic Cytoplasm | Mitochondrion | Aerobic Bacterium |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ribosome Size | $80\text{S}$ | $70\text{S}$ | $70\text{S}$ |
| DNA Configuration | Linear | Circular | Circular |

![Comparative Biology Diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-biology-new-c9fdac86-981b-4d2a-98fe-700cda62c2e2-1771903319952/6072c90e-bbac-4a62-baf1-f845bc826f7e.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.2 Cell structure > A2.2.12  Origin of eukaryotic cells by endosymbiosis (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.2.12  Origin of eukaryotic cells by endosymbiosis (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks to deduce the evolutionary origin of mitochondria using evidence such as 70S ribosomes and circular DNA—classic endosymbiotic theory evidence.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C1.2 Cell respiration > C1.2.6 Variables affecting the rate of cell respiration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C1.2.6 Variables affecting the rate of cell respiration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) predicts the effect of inhibiting 70S ribosomes on aerobic respiration rate in eukaryotic cells via impacts on mitochondrial protein synthesis, which affects respiration rate (a variable affecting respiration).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D1.2 Protein synthesis > D1.2.6 Roles of mRNA, ribosomes and tRNA in translation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D1.2.6 Roles of mRNA, ribosomes and tRNA in translation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) involves antibiotics inhibiting 70S ribosomes, linking to the role of ribosomes in translation/protein synthesis within mitochondria.

parts.content: Using the evidence provided, deduce the evolutionary origin of the mitochondrion according to the endosymbiotic theory.
parts.markscheme:
- the mitochondrion originated from an ancestral free-living prokaryote/aerobic bacterium; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- this is supported by the mitochondrion possessing $70\text{S}$ ribosomes and circular DNA, which match the traits of the bacterium rather than the eukaryotic host's cytoplasm; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- mitochondria retain some of their own genes and protein-synthesis machinery consistent with a prokaryotic ancestor; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- similarity to bacteria supports engulfment/retention as an endosymbiont; <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Antibiotics such as chloramphenicol specifically inhibit the function of $70\text{S}$ ribosomes. Predict and explain the likely effect of this antibiotic on the rate of aerobic respiration within a eukaryotic cell.
parts.markscheme:
- the rate of aerobic respiration/ATP production would decrease; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- inhibition of the mitochondrion's $70\text{S}$ ribosomes prevents the synthesis of specific proteins/enzymes required for the electron transport chain/Krebs cycle; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- reduced production of mitochondrial respiratory proteins lowers oxidative phosphorylation efficiency; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- fewer functional electron carriers/enzymes leads to reduced proton pumping and ATP synthase activity; <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Natural selection is the differential survival and reproduction of individuals due to differences in phenotype.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.1 Natural selection > D4.1.1 Natural selection as the mechanism driving evolutionary change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.1.1 Natural selection as the mechanism driving evolutionary change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks to discuss the process of natural selection (variation, differential survival, and inheritance) as the primary mechanism for change.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.1 Origins of cells (HL) > A2.1.3 Challenge of explaining the spontaneous origin of cells
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.3 Challenge of explaining the spontaneous origin of cells
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Protocells are early precursors in the origin of life, and the transition from pre-biotic chemistry to self-sustaining cells is covered under the origin of cells.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A4.1 Evolution and speciation > A4.1.9 Adaptive radiation as a source of biodiversity (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A4.1.9 Adaptive radiation as a source of biodiversity (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks how these processes led to the current 'diversity of life', which is the result of adaptive radiation and speciation over time.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.1 Origins of cells (HL) > A2.1.5 Spontaneous formation of vesicles by coalescence of fatty acids into spherical bilayers
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.5 Spontaneous formation of vesicles by coalescence of fatty acids into spherical bilayers
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This topic specifically mentions the spontaneous formation of vesicles from fatty acids, which describes the physical structure of the protocells mentioned in the question.

parts.content: Outline how natural selection could have acted on early protocells.
parts.markscheme:
- Natural selection would favour protocells with heritable traits that increased survival and reproduction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Protocells with advantageous traits would leave more offspring so the traits increase in frequency over generations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Variation between protocells (e.g. due to copying errors/mutations in genetic material) provides the raw material for selection <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Environmental pressures act as selective agents, so different conditions favour different protocell variants <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how variation in protocell structure and metabolism could have increased their survival.
parts.markscheme:
- More stable/less permeable membranes (e.g. lipid bilayers) would better maintain internal conditions and reduce loss of contents <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ability to take up and use available resources (e.g. substrates/ions) would increase growth and persistence <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Development of more efficient metabolic pathways/enzymes would increase energy yield from available chemicals <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Protocells able to tolerate environmental stress (e.g. temperature, pH, salinity) would survive and reproduce more successfully <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Any linked explanation that structural/metabolic differences confer a reproductive advantage <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how natural selection acting on early protocells could ultimately lead to the diversity of life observed today.
parts.markscheme:
- Heritable variation arises continuously (e.g. mutation/copying errors in nucleic acids) producing new phenotypes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Protocells with advantageous phenotypes are more likely to survive and reproduce so their alleles increase in frequency <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Different environments/selective pressures favour different variants, leading to divergence among populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accumulation of genetic changes over long timescales can result in increasingly complex cellular systems (e.g. improved replication, metabolism, regulation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Selection can favour cooperation/compartmentalisation (e.g. internal membranes, symbiosis) increasing complexity of cells <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Over time, divergence and increasing complexity can lead to speciation and adaptive radiation into many niches <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cumulative outcome is a wide range of organisms with different adaptations, producing today’s biodiversity <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for relevant examples of selective pressures or adaptations if provided</note>

questions.specification:
The diagram shows a protocell. What key characteristic defines a protocell as a precursor to living cells?

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f7fa6628-997b-4913-ac4e-8d46f3317a90)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.1 Origins of cells (HL) > A2.1.5 Spontaneous formation of vesicles by coalescence of fatty acids into spherical bilayers
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.1.5 Spontaneous formation of vesicles by coalescence of fatty acids into spherical bilayers
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about the defining characteristic of a protocell as a precursor to life. In the context of the origins of life, a protocell is fundamentally a vesicle formed by the spontaneous assembly of lipid (fatty acid) bilayers, which provides the necessary compartmentalization for subsequent metabolic and genetic processes. This is specifically described in topic A2.1.5.

options.content: The ability to maintain an internal environment distinct from the surroundings
options.markscheme: A protocell is primarily defined by compartmentalization that separates internal chemistry from the external environment.
While some protocells could grow and divide, division is not the defining feature; compartmentalization is more fundamental.
Early protocells are hypothesized to have used RNA or simpler genetic systems; DNA replication evolved later.
Mitochondria evolved much later via endosymbiosis in eukaryotes and are not a feature of protocells.

questions.specification:
A historical experiment conducted by Louis Pasteur tested the hypothesis of spontaneous generation. He used two swan-neck flasks containing boiled nutrient broth:
- Flask A: The swan-neck was intact, allowing air to enter but preventing dust and microbes from reaching the broth.
- Flask B: The swan-neck was broken, allowing dust and microbes to enter the broth directly.
After several days, the results showed:

| Flask | Microbial growth observed? |
|---|---|
| Flask A (intact neck) | No |
| Flask B (broken neck) | Yes |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.1 Cell and nuclear division > D2.1.1 Generation of new cells in living organisms by cell division
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.1.1 Generation of new cells in living organisms by cell division
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks how Pasteur’s swan-neck flask experiment supports the principle that cells (microorganisms) arise from pre-existing cells rather than spontaneously, aligning with the core idea of biogenesis/cell theory.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A2.2 Cell structure > A2.2.7  Processes of life in unicellular organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A2.2.7  Processes of life in unicellular organisms
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Pasteur’s experiment involves microbial growth (unicellular organisms) in nutrient broth; interpreting growth vs no growth touches on life processes in unicellular organisms (microbes), though this is secondary to the biogenesis/cell theory point.

parts.content: Using the results in the table, explain how Pasteur’s experiment provided evidence against spontaneous generation and supported the cell theory.
parts.markscheme:
Award marks for the following:
- States that microorganisms did not arise spontaneously in the broth; growth occurred only when contamination by pre-existing microorganisms was possible. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links to cell theory by stating that cells (microorganisms) come from pre-existing cells (biogenesis), not from non-living matter. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Acceptable supporting detail (do not award additional marks beyond 2):
- In Flask A, airborne microbes/dust were trapped in the swan neck so broth was not contaminated.
- In Flask B, breaking the neck allowed microbes/dust to enter, leading to microbial growth.

Total: <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Discuss one other piece of scientific evidence that supports that all cells come from pre-existing cells.
parts.markscheme:
Any one of the following pieces of evidence:

- Microscopic observations of cell division (e.g. Remak/Virchow) showing cells form by division of existing cells (mitosis/binary fission). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how observing division demonstrates that new cells arise from pre-existing cells rather than appearing spontaneously. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**OR**

- Experimental evidence of regulated cell division (e.g. observations/experiments showing mitosis/meiosis/binary fission produces daughter cells from a parent cell). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that this supports cell theory because new cells are produced only via division of existing cells. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**OR**

- States that there is no verified observation/experiment of a living cell arising from non-living material under natural conditions (absence of evidence for spontaneous generation/cell formation). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that this supports the conclusion that cells come from pre-existing cells (biogenesis). <marks>1 mark</marks>

Total: <marks>2 marks</marks>

questions.specification:
The graph shows the mitotic index measured in the intestinal crypts of a mammal at various distances from the base of the crypt.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-biology-new-fc63abf8-9592-4733-b2f6-79708573bdfe-1771815806714/9e588adc-7499-404a-9ddb-63110f62ad00.jpeg)

What can be deduced from the graph?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.1 Cell and nuclear division > D2.1.13 Phases of the cell cycle (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.1.13 Phases of the cell cycle (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to deduce information about the cell cycle (mitosis versus interphase) based on the mitotic index. This falls directly under understanding the phases and proportions of the cell cycle.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D2.1 Cell and nuclear division > D2.1.12 Cell proliferation for growth, cell replacement and tissue repair (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D2.1.12 Cell proliferation for growth, cell replacement and tissue repair (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question uses the example of intestinal crypts, which is a classic biological context for studying cell proliferation for tissue replacement and repair.

options.content: At $10\ \mu m$ from the base, the majority of the epithelial cells are undergoing mitosis.
The cells at the base of the crypt have a shorter total cell cycle duration than those at $100\ \mu m$.
The total number of cells observed in the sample decreases as the distance from the base increases.
The percentage of cells in interphase is higher at a distance of $120\ \mu m$ than at $40\ \mu m$ from the base.
